blocks.text: To: A Plus Home Health Systems, LLC
The information contained in this fax transmission is intended only for the individual(s) named above.
Such information is confidential and may be legally privileged. If you have received this fax transmission
in error, please notify me immediately by using the telephone number provided above so that / may
arrange for this fax transmission to be returned to me or destroyed. If the recipient of this fax
transmission is not the individual(s) named above, such recipient is hereby notified that this fax
transmission may not be copied, disseminated, distributed or otherwise disclosed to others.
If unable to contact the sender of this fax, please contact the Privacy Officer, Bob Breighner, at 888-735-
770 E Warm Springs Rd Ste 250 Las Vegas NV 89119-4380

Reason for Admission: Metabolic encephalopathy with functional decline
History of present illness: Hui Y. Hu is a 63-year-old female with history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia,
dementia, Parkinson's disease, depression, anxiety, and tardive dyskinesia who presents after recent
Hu, Hui Y (MR # 7582 / ACCT # 10959) Printed at 3/30/2026 11:42 AM EDT
hospitalization from 3/7/2026 to 3/15/2026 for severe hyponatremia, NSTEMI, and aspiration pneumonia. She
initially presented with 2 days of dizziness and multiple falls and was found to have sodium of 112 requiring
ICU admission and DDAVP treatment. Hospital course was complicated by NSTEMI and aspiration
pneumonia. Since discharge, patient continues to have confusion and memory impairment, unable to recall
daily activities or current month, though she knows her name and year. Husband reports decline compared to
prior baseline over the past 3-6 months, with acute worsening after hospitalization. Patient has flat affect but
follows commands and is alert and oriented x3 at times. She is being transferred to inpatient rehab for
functional decline and ongoing cognitive deficits.
Independent to modified independent with ambulation, able to communicate and oriented to basic information
per husband, walking daily, improved mobility with treatment of tardive dyskinesia
Requires assistance with mobility and ADLs, impaired cognition with memory deficits and disorientation,
decreased safety awareness, increased fall risk
Discharge home with husband with support and services
History reviewed. No pertinent surgical history.
History reviewed. No pertinent family history.

Additional Comments: I have personally viewed and interpreted the patient's lab results and discussed with
General: No acute distress, Well developed, Well nourished.
Eye: Pupils are equal, round and reactive to light, Extraocular movements are intact, Normal conjunctiva.
HENT: Normocephalic, Normal hearing, Oral mucosa is moist, No pharyngeal erythema.
Neck: Supple, Non-tender, No carotid bruit, No jugular venous distention.
Respiratory: Lungs are clear to auscultation, Respirations are non-labored, Breath sounds are equal.
Cardiovascular: Normal rate, Regular rhythm, No murmur, No gallop.
Gastrointestinal: Soft, Non-tender, Non-distended, Normal bowel sounds.
Neurologic: Alert, Oriented, No focal deficits. 3+/5 RUE, 3+/5 LUE, 3/5 RLE, 3/5 LLE
Mental status/ Cognition: Alert, Oriented X 3 with intermittent confusion and memory impairment.
Musculoskeletal: Normal range of motion, Decreased strength, No tenderness, No swelling.
Sensory/Tone/Reflexes: Normal sensation, Decreased muscle strength, Normal muscle stretch reflexes,
Hu, Hui Y (MR # 7582 / ACCT # 10959) Printed at 3/30/2026 11:42 AM EDT
ID: Hui Y. Hu is a 63-year-old female with history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, dementia, Parkinson's
disease, depression, anxiety, and tardive dyskinesia who presents after recent hospitalization from 3/7/2026 to
3/15/2026 for severe hyponatremia, NSTEMI, and aspiration pneumonia. She initially presented with 2 days of
dizziness and multiple falls and was found to have sodium of 112 requiring ICU admission and DDAVP
treatment. Hospital course was complicated by NSTEMI and aspiration pneumonia. Since discharge, patient
continues to have confusion and memory impairment, unable to recall daily activities or current month, though
she knows her name and year. Husband reports decline compared to prior baseline over the past 3-6 months,
with acute worsening after hospitalization. Patient has flat affect but follows commands and is alert and
oriented x3 at times. She is being transferred to inpatient rehab for functional decline and ongoing cognitive
Rehab Diagnosis: Metabolic encephalopathy with functional decline (RIG: 02.1)
Barriers to rehabilitation and going home:
Low level of function on admission
Medical and functional conditions that meet necessity of Inpatient Rehab Facility (IRF) level of care:
Patient requires acute medical oversight for current medical condition. Patient will require monitoring of vitals
and labs, pain and medication management. Patient will require dietary support. Will require intensive PT, OT,
and ST due to impaired mobility, impaired ADL management, balance and strength deficits. Will require home
safety training and education, may need family training and other needs as appropriate per POC. Patient will
have 24 hr medical management by a PM&R Physician and care to include a weekly interdisciplinary team
conference. Due to mobility and ADL impairments patient is at risk for worsening of primary condition,
increased risk of infection, DVT, PE, muscle weakness, falls, skin breakdown, aspiration and other
*Metabolic encephalopathy: impairments in mobility and ADLs; monitor neurological status and mental status
changes; patient will require neuromuscular reeducation and cognitive rehabilitation; monitor progress in
aggressive therapeutic program; monitor bowel, bladder and skin; dietary support and patient and family
*Dysphagia: will need SLP swallow eval given recent aspiration pneumonia, modified diet as tolerated and
safe, MBS as needed and nutritional supplementation.
Hu, Hui Y (MR # 7582 / ACCT # 10959) Printed at 3/30/2026 11:42 AM EDT
*Acute Hypoxia/Aspiration pneumonia: monitor respiratory status and oxygen saturation; currently on room air
but at risk for decompensation; monitor for signs of recurrent aspiration or pulmonary complications; continue
antibiotic management; supportive respiratory care as needed.
*Urinary retention: monitor for urinary dysfunction given cognitive impairment and history of recurrent UTIs;
monitor output and signs of retention or infection.
*Parkinson's disease: will need careful monitoring of medication timing and effectiveness; monitor for changes
in motor symptoms, rigidity, and mobility; consider Neurology follow-up.
*Autonomic dysfunction: monitor hemodynamic status given Parkinson's disease and recent hospitalization;
*Uncontrolled HTN: monitor BP values; continue medication management; patient and family education;
*CAD/NSTEMI: patient will need to be monitored for cardiac symptoms, hemodynamic stability, and signs of
*Hyperlipidemia: monitor lab values; continue statin therapy; dietary consult and patient education.
*Leukocytosis: mild leukocytosis; monitor labs and signs/symptoms of infection given recent pneumonia.
*Insomnia: monitor sleep wake cycle; medication management as needed.
*Functional and cognitive impairments currently at a level unsafe for discharge home: evidence for intense
*Back pain: monitor as needed though no acute complaints.
*Fall risk: patient will need constant monitoring because of reduced endurance, weakness, cognitive
impairment and fatigue; patient is a significant fall risk; at risk for pressure injuries.
*Electrolyte abnormality: history of severe hyponatremia; will need close monitoring of electrolytes to prevent
*VTE: medication management with aspirin and encourage frequent mobility.
I concur with the RIG code as listen in the pre-evaluation. After my evaluation and comparison to pre-
evaluation the patient is as expected. Rehabilitative services will include PT/OT/ST 3 hours daily, 5 days per
week. This patient will benefit from this inpatient rehabilitation program to maximize independence, reduce
caregiver burden or needs and reduce risk of hospital readmission.
PT/OT goals are to improve balance, strength, endurance to achieve a CARE TOOL level 6 for transfers, basic
ST goals are to improve communication, cognition, safety, speech, swallow, and awareness.
Rehabilitative nurses will work with doctors on pain management, vital sign stability, bowel and bladder health,
nutritional support and reinforcing the therapy gains made by the therapy disciplines.
Prognosis: At the current time, this inpatient hospital rehabilitation stay is medically necessary to achieve
important health and functional goals. The patient requires frequent physician visits, 24-hour rehabilitation
nursing, and a coordinated intensive rehabilitation program as described above to address complex medical,
nursing, and rehabilitation needs. The patient has a good prognosis for benefiting from this program and
- Consult SLP for cognitive eval and dysphagia
ACP discussed and documented, ACP and/or proxy decision maker documented within the medical record.
Hu, Hui Y (MR # 7582 / ACCT # 10959) Printed at 3/30/2026 11:42 AM EDT
DVT ppx: SCDs, will follow IM management
Advanced Care Planning was discussed with the patient or the MDPOA. They were present and able to make
decisions. The conversation was offered as voluntary and the patient/MDPOA was given an opportunity to
decline the services. The patient/MDPOA chose to have the discussion and in the course of discussion the
risks, benefits, and alternatives to the advance directive options were reviewed. The patient's values and
overall goals for treatment were discussed. The patient's options for palliative and end-of-life care, should the
time arise, and how to avoid hospital readmissions were discussed and reviewed. The Nevada POLST form
was reviewed with the patient/ MDPOA. The form was signed along with a documented change in health status
and/or wishes regarding end-of-life care. 16 minutes or more was spent discussing Advance Care Planning.
This case involved review of data, evaluation, examination, consideration of differential diagnosis and
management options, a formulation of the management plan based on critical analysis and processing of
available data, patient, family and/or caregivers education regarding underlying medical conditions, and
coordination of care with nursing staff and other providers. The total time spent was more than 70 minutes.

63-year-old female with history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, dementia, Parkinson's disease, depression,
anxiety, and tardive dyskinesia who presents after recent hospitalization from 3/7/2026 to 3/15/2026 for severe
hyponatremia, NSTEMI, and aspiration pneumonia. She initially presented with 2 days of dizziness and
multiple falls and was found to have sodium of 112 requiring ICU admission and DDAVP treatment. Hospital
course was complicated by NSTEMI and aspiration pneumonia. Since discharge, patient continues to have
confusion and memory impairment, unable to recall daily activities or current month, though she knows her
name and year. Husband reports decline compared to prior baseline over the past 3-6 months, with acute
worsening after hospitalization. Transferred to Dignity rehab 3/20/2026.
Hu, Hui Y (MR # 7582 / ACCT # 10959) Printed at 3/30/2026 11:42 AM EDT

- PT /OT for strengthening, balance, mobilization, ADL's
-SLP evaluating swallow given history of aspiration
-MBS performed 3/23, no evidence of dysphagia, continue regular diet
According to my review of the medical conditions and functional status, the patient is progressing toward DC
Largely supervision for all ADLs and mobility, on track for discharge
Team Interaction: Discussed with case manager
55 minutes was spent on today's encounter which included evaluation and examination of the patient,
discussion with the patient regarding medications for discharge as well as completing medication reconciliation
and electronic prescribing of medications, discussions with other health care providers including case manager
and nursing staff, and documentation in the electronic medical record.
No treatment plan modifications are necessary at this time. Continue current rehabilitation program.
